title: Blondies
subtitle: Brown-sugar traybake with white chocolate
excerpt: A vanilla-and-brown-sugar traybake that is to chocolate brownies what white chocolate is to dark: richer and sweeter, with a dense, fudgy crumb. No cocoa. White chocolate chips optional.
sourceNotes: The blondie is documented in American domestic cookery from the 1940s as a brownie variant made without cocoa. The original recipe uses brown sugar rather than white, which gives the characteristic butterscotch flavour through Maillard browning of the sugar. This version follows the mid-century American home-baking formula.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap individual squares. Freeze for up to 3 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps well for 4 days in a tin. Slightly better on day two when the flavour has settled.

glossaryTerms.definition: The caramel-like taste produced when brown sugar is heated with butter. Brown sugar contains molasses, which caramelises at a lower temperature than white sugar, producing a richer, darker flavour.

content.text: A blondie is a brownie made without chocolate. The sweetness is brown sugar — light brown for a milder result, dark brown for a deeper
. The only real technique is the bake time: take the blondie out while the centre still moves slightly. A fully-set blondie in the tin becomes a dry blondie on the plate.
Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Grease a 20×20 cm tin and line with baking paper.
Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} in a medium saucepan over low heat. Remove from the heat and stir in the {{light-brown-sugar}} until dissolved. Leave to cool for 10 minutes.

Beat in the {{free-range-eggs}} one at a time, then the {{vanilla-extract}}. Stir in the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{fine-sea-salt}} until just combined. Fold in the {{white-chocolate-chips}} if using.

Pour the batter into the tin and spread to the corners. Bake for 22–25 minutes. The blondie is ready when the edges are set and the centre gives a very slight wobble when you shake the tin. A skewer through the centre should come out with damp crumbs rather than clean. Do not overbake.
Cool in the tin for 30 minutes before cutting. The blondie firms as it cools and cuts cleanly only when it is at room temperature.
